coredata.dc:title: Ethylenediamine-assisted hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for sensitive biosensing and bioimaging
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Niu, Wen-Jun
Li, Ye
Zhu, Rong-Hui
Shan, Dan
Fan, Yan-Rong
Zhang, Xue-Ji
coredata.dc:description: In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield (46.2%) were successfully synthesized through one-pot hydrothermal carbonization approach assisted by ethylenediamine as surface passivation agent, wherein alanine acted as a single carbon source, ethylenediamine as the surface passivation agent. The obtained biocompatible N-CQDs could effectively label both the MCF-7 cell membranes and the nucleus, suggesting the potential application in biolabeling and bioimaging. Furthermore, the addition of dihydronicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH) could dramatically quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs. Therefore, a novel protocol for NADH determination was established and its mechanism was discussed. This proposed fluorescence sensor displayed an enhanced performance for NADH determination with the linear range up to 80μM and the detection limit down to 25.1nM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
Fluorescent probe
NADH
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cytotoxicity by introducing N-CQDs in cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Processing diagram for the synthesis of ethylenediamine-passivated CQDs.
LR- (a) and HR-TEM TEM (b, c, d) images of the as-synthesized N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). The inset of (a) shows the particle size distribution histogram.
XPS survey spectrum of the N-doped carbon quantum dots (a), high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (b), N1s (c) and O1s (d).

a) FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs. b) UV–vis absorption (—), PL excitation (- - -), and emission (----) spectra of N-CQDs in aqueous solutions. Insets show photographs of N-CQDs in aqueous solution under visible (left) and UV (right) light. c) Excitation-dependent PL of N-CQDs. d) A typical time-resolved fluorescence-decay curve of N-CQDs (Ex=320nm) measured at 390nm.
Cell viability of the A549 and MCF-7 cells after incubation with the photoluminescent N-CQDs for 24h (mean±SD, n =3).

Fluorescence microscopy images of MCF-7 cells labeled with N-CQDs: a) the bright-field image of MCF-7 cells; b) excitation by UV light (λ ex =365nm); c) excitation by blue light (λ ex =460nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of the article).
FL responses of the N-CQDs to 1mM NADH and 1mM other interferential biological molecules.
Schematic illustration of the mechanism involved in the fluorescence quenching caused by the presence of NADH.

a) Representative FL emission spectra of N-CQDs in the presence of increasing NADH concentrations (0–1mM) in PBS buffer, b) The relationship between (I 0 − I)/I 0 and NADH from 0 to 1mM. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements. Inset is a linear region. I and I 0 are the FL intensities of N-CQDs at 320nm in the presence and absence of NADH, respectively.

head.ce:title: Ethylenediamine-assisted hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for sensitive biosensing and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Biological Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Biological Engineering
sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield (46.2%) were successfully synthesized through one-pot hydrothermal carbonization approach assisted by ethylenediamine as surface passivation agent, wherein alanine acted as a single carbon source, ethylenediamine as the surface passivation agent. The obtained biocompatible N-CQDs could effectively label both the MCF-7 cell membranes and the nucleus, suggesting the potential application in biolabeling and bioimaging. Furthermore, the addition of dihydronicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH) could dramatically quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs. Therefore, a novel protocol for NADH determination was established and its mechanism was discussed. This proposed fluorescence sensor displayed an enhanced performance for NADH determination with the linear range up to 80μM and the detection limit down to 25.1nM.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) emerging as a new member of the carbon-based nanomaterials, similar to carbon nanotubes [1], fullerenes [2] and graphene [3], have drawn considerable attention in fluorescent sensor [4–10], biological imaging [11–15], drug delivery [16,17], photocatalysis [18,19] and LED device [20,21]. Simultaneously, many strategies including arc-discharge [22], laser-ablation [12,23], acidic oxidation [24–27], electrochemical synthesis [13,19,28], microwave synthesis [29–31], ultrasonic treatment [32,33] and hydrothermal carbonization [11,34–36] have been developed to synthesize CQDs since their serendipitous discovery by Xu group [22] in purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes.

Unfortunately, the preparations of CQDs through the above-mentioned methods usually involve expensive precursors, intricate processes or low quantum yields (QY). Therefore, it is necessary to develop a facile and cost-effective protocol for the CQDs synthesis. Since the nitrogen can provide the CQDs a new kind of surface state to enhance the fluorescence intensity [34,36], significant efforts have been devoted to synthesize the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). For instance, amino acids [29,37] or proteins [11] were selected as the ideal molecular precursors for synthesizing N-CQDs due to their abundance, cost effectiveness and biocompatibility. On the other hand, to dramatically improve the QY and biocompatibility of the as-synthesized CQDs, a number of post-treatment steps have been reported lately [12–15,30]. However, the post synthesis treatments, such as surface passivation scheme involving polyethylene glycol (PEG) [14,15] or polyethylenimine (PEI) [30], are time consuming and arduous. It is debatable whether or not the small organic compounds (ethylenediamine or other homologues) would be a promising alternative.

Meanwhile, as a fluorescent probe, high fluorescence quantum yield, low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility are one of the most critical requirements for sensitive biosensing and bioimaging. For example, carbon dots as a non-enzymatic sensor were applied to blood glucose [6], dopamine [7], glutathione [8], tetracyclines [9] and so on. As a nontoxic and high-performance fluorescence imaging agent, the CQDs have been used for optical imaging in vitro [11–15,38] and in vivo [14,39–41]. Nevertheless, some of these recently reported studies still show negative results due to poor properties of CQDs. Much work is still needed to exploit these nanomaterials in sensitive biosensing and bioimaging.

Herein, we developed a facile and economic synthetic approach for the preparation of N-CQDs via hydrothermal carbonization alanine and ethylenediamine for the first time. The formation of N-CQDs and the surface passivation by ethylenediamine were accomplished simultaneously in one pot. The spectral properties and biocompatibility of the N-CQDs are studied in detail. Furthermore, a novel N-CQDs sensor for NADH is proposed based on the effective fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs and the quenching mechanism is discussed as well.

Alanine (Ala) and ethylenediamine were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide reduced disodium salt hydrate (NADH), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), nicotinamide, d-biotin, adenosine 5′-triphosphate disodium salt hydrate (ATP), glutathione, folic acid and vitamin B1/B2/B6 were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Sunshine Biotechnology Company (Nanjing, China). All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was 0.1M K2HPO4 and KH2PO4 and its pH was adjusted with H3PO4 or KOH solutions. Twice-distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

The N-CQDs were prepared by a modified hydrothermal carbonization protocol [34,35]. In a typical one-step synthesis, 2.673g alanine was dissolved into 30.0mL of ultrapure water, followed by adding 500μL ethylenediamine under stirring. Then, the solution was transferred into the Teflon-lined autoclave chamber (50.0mL) and heated to 200°C for 6h. After the reaction, the reactors were cooled to room temperature. The product, which was brown-black and transparent, was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 MWCO) for 24h. Finally, the solution was dried at 45°C by rotary evaporators to obtain solid powders. The major steps and mechanism involved in the synthesis of N-CQDs are illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The morphology and size of N-CQDs were analyzed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, Philips TECNAI-12) under the accelerating voltage of 300kV. The samples were prepared by the deposition of 10μL N-CQDs aqueous solutions on a copper grid coated with ultra-thin films of carbon, while the solvent was removed by evaporation in air at room temperature. The crystalline structure and the surface functional properties of N-CQDs were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., USA). Infrared spectra were measured on a pressed KBr pellet employing a TENSOR Model 27 FT-IR spectrometer. The fluorescence and the absorption spectra were measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Analytical Instruments, FLS920) and UV–vis spectrophotometry (UV-3600, Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were conducted with the time-correlated single-photon counting technique on the combined steady-state and lifetime spectrometer (Edinburgh Analytical Instruments, FLS920).

The quantum yields (ϕ) of the N-CQDs were calculated following slope method [35], where the quinine sulfate (0.1mol/L H2SO4 as solvent; QY=0.54) were chosen as standards. Specifically, we compared the absorbance (several values, less than 0.1 at excitation wavelength) and the corresponding integrated photoluminescence intensity of N-CQDs aqueous solutions with quinine sulfate references at their optimal excitation wavelengths, respectively. Then, the integrated photoluminescence intensity against the absorbance was plotted and a linear regression was attained, where the slope of the curves could be obtained. Finally, we used the equation as follows:
ϕ x
=
ϕ
st
K x
K
st
η x 2
η
st
2
where ϕ is the QY, K is the slope determined from the curves, η is the refractive index of the solvent (both are 1.33). The subscript st and x are denoted to quinine standard and N-CQDs aqueous solutions, respectively.

The cytotoxicity of the N-CQDs was evaluated by the MTT assay. Briefly, human lung cancer A549 and human breast cancer MCF-7 cells were first cultured with high-glucose DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin/streptomycin solution in an incubator (37°C, 5% CO2) until cell counts were 4×103 in each well. Then, the culture medium was replaced with 100μL of DMEM containing the N-CQDs at different doses (0, 10, 25, 50, 100, 200, 400μg/mL) to seed for another 24h. After that, 20μL of 5mg/mL MTT solution was added to each cell well and the cells were further incubated for 4h, followed by removing the culture medium with MTT, 150μL of DMSO was added. The resulting mixture was shaken about 10min at room temperature and the optical density (OD) was measured at 490nm with a microplate reader. Finally, the cell viability was estimated as the following equation:
Cell
viability
( % ) =
OD
Treated
OD
Control
× 100 %
where ODControl was obtained in the absence of N-CQDs, ODTreated was obtained in the presence of N-CQDs.

MCF-7 cells were cultured firstly with high-glucose DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin/streptomycin solution in an incubator (37°C, 5% CO2) until the counting of cells were 1×105 per well. Then, the culture medium was replaced with 100μL of culture medium containing the N-CQDs at 200μg/mL to seed for another 24h. Prior to using 4% paraformaldehyde, the cells were fixed on a slide for inspection with a fluorescence microscope, the excess N-CQDs were removed by washing three times with warm PBS. Then, the cells were imaged under a fluorescence microscope (Zeiss Imager. M2, Germany) with UV light (λ ex =365nm) and blue light (λ ex =460nm).

The detection of NADH was performed in PBS (0.1M, pH 7.4). In a typical assay, 50μL of purified N-CQDs was placed in a series of 5.0mL colorimetric tubes. Then, 3.0mL of NADH in varying concentrations was added to each tube. After an interval of 10min, the fluorescence spectra of sample were recorded at the emission wavelengths (λ em =390nm) under the optimal excitation wavelengths (λ ex =320nm), respectively. The selectivity for NADH was confirmed by adding other coenzyme instead of NADH in a similar way. All experiments were performed at room temperature. For human serum assay, an ultrafiltration process was conducted to remove large molecules and proteins by using a 3kDa ultrafiltration tube. Then, different concentrations of NADH were spiked in the treated human serum samples and analyzed under the conditions identical to those described above.

The morphology, size and microstructure of the as-prepared N-CQDs were characterized by TEM (Fig. 2 ). As shown in Fig. 2a, the TEM image suggests that the N-CQDs possess a uniform spherical shape with narrow size distribution. The inset of Fig. 2 displays the size distribution of the products, which agrees well with the Gaussian distribution. It can be definitely seen that the average size of the as-synthesized N-CQDs is about 8nm by statistical analysis of more than five hundred particles. The structural details and the atomic lattice fringes could be observed clearly in HR-TEM (Fig. 2b–d). As shown in Fig. 2c and d, both lattice spacing about 0.178 and 0.238nm are very close to the (004) interlayer spacing and (100) in-plane lattice spacing of graphite respectively [6,34], indicating that the N-CQDs exhibit a high-crystalline graphitic structure.

The surface functional groups and elemental states of the N-CQDs were characterized by XPS. The survey spectrum (Fig. 3a) of the N-CQDs shows three typical peaks of C1s, N1s, and O1s, with the corresponding content of each element is 61.1%, 24.6% and 14.3% as displayed in the inset of Fig. 3a. Significantly, the content of nitrogen is comparable to these nitrogen-doped CQDs as reported earlier [34,36]. The spectrum of C1s in the N-CQDs (Fig. 3b) can be subdivided into several single peaks that correspond to CC (284.5eV), CN (286.0eV), CO (286.5eV), and CN/CO (288.0eV) functional groups. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) reveals two relative nitrogen species of CNC (399.5eV) and NH (401.5eV), which further verifies the as-synthesized CQDs are nitrogen-doped CQDs. Moreover, the spectrum of O1s (Fig. 3d) further confirms these observations with two characteristic oxygen states of CO (531.6eV) and COH/COC (533.0eV) [11,34,36], which have been observed in the case of C1s.

Meanwhile, FT-IR was applied to investigate the surface groups, structure and components of the as-prepared N-CQDs [8,36]. As shown in Fig. 4a, several functional groups as OH, NH, COH, CO, CC, CN and CO groups exist on the surface of N-CQDs. Specifically, the existence of a broad peak centered at 3440cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of OH and NH. The sharp peak at 1200cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibrations of COH, suggesting that there are plenty of hydroxyl groups and amine groups on the surface of N-CQDs. In addition, the peak at 1610 and 1470cm−1 is associated with CO, CC stretching vibrations and CN bending vibrations, while the peak at 1045cm−1 is the CO bending vibrations. The FT-IR results are in consistence with those of the XPS investigation.

To study the spectral properties of the N-CQDs, the UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence (PL) emission and excitation spectra were acquired from the aqueous dispersion of N-CQDs as illustrated in Fig. 4b. A strong absorption peak at 320nm was observed in the UV–vis absorption spectrum, which can be ascribed to the n–π* transition of CO groups on the surface of N-CQDs [35]. Meanwhile, the optimal excitation wavelengths (λ ex =320nm) and emission wavelengths (λ em =390nm) of the as-synthesized N-CQDs can be obtained from the PL emission and excitation spectra, respectively. From the photographs depicted in the inset of Fig. 4b, the aqueous solution of the diluted N-CQDs is pale yellow under visible light but exhibits a bright blue color under the illumination of a hand-held UV lamp (365nm).

Usually, excitation-dependent PL behaviors of N-CQDs reflect effect from particles of different sizes in sample and a distribution of different surface states [22–24,42]. It is worth studying whether the as-synthesized N-CQDs showed excitation-dependent emission character. As depicted in Fig. 4c, with the excitation wavelengths varying from 310 to 480nm, the emission peak shifted to longer wavelength and the fluorescence intensity decreased, suggesting the similar fluorescent properties. The properties may be contributed to the quantum dimensions affecting the band gap of N-CQDs [24,42]. Furthermore, the fluorescence quantum yields (ϕ) with ethylenediamine-passivated N-CQDs (46.2%) were calculated by slope method [35], using the quinine sulfate (0.1mol/L H2SO4 as solvent; QY=0.54) as standards. Interestingly, compared with other reported CQDs synthesized by amino acids [29,37] and proteins [11], this is the highest value recorded for fluorescent carbon-based materials, which is almost equal to these CQDs synthesized by arc-discharge [22], or laser-ablation [12,23].

Although the exact mechanism of PL is still not clear, it has been well reported that the surface energy trapped on the bare CQDs surface should have more prominent impact on the photophysical properties of CQDs [22–24,42]. Specifically, the PL of the CQDs should be attributed to the radiative recombination of electrons and holes trapped on the CQDs surface. Therefore, it is reasonable that the high fluorescence quantum yield of ethylenediamine-passivated N-CQDs may be attributed to the interplay between the holes trapped and the passivation of the CQDs [23,39,42]. Moreover, the requirement for surface passivation to dramatically improve the QY is apparently shared by the carbon dots and the silicon nanocrystals, where a widely accepted mechanism for luminescence emission is the radiative recombination of excitons [23,39].

To gain more insight into the origin of the PL behavior for explaining the high QY of N-CQDs, more accurately, the reaction conditions such as the concentration of reactants, temperature and time were found to be very important. Tables 1S, 2S and 3S summarizes the rules determined for raising the QY of N-CQDs. At optimal excitation wavelengths (λ ex =320nm), as Table 1S shows, the observed quantum yields of ethylenediamine-passivated N-CQDs (46.2%) were substantially higher than those using alanine (7.48%) or ethylenediamine (3.00%) alone, which further proved the possible mechanism of PL as mentioned above. However, the QY of N-CQDs decreased gradually along with the concentration of ethylenediamine increased continually. The exact mechanism is still under investigation, but the probable reason might be the excess ethylenediamine, which will invade the remainder holes trapped on the CQDs surface [23,39,42]. Meanwhile, Suitable reaction temperature (Table 2S) and time (Table 3S) is very necessary to promote QY of N-CQDs.

Simultaneously, as seen in Fig. 4d, the decay trace was fitted using biexponential functions as follows: Y(t)= A 1exp(−t/τ 1)+ A 2exp(−t/τ 2), where A 1 and A 2 are the fractional contributions of time-resolved decay lifetime of τ 1 and τ 2, respectively. Interestingly, the biexponential behavior of the N-CQDs suggests that two different emissive sites are present and the fluorescence state is due to the graphite center (or conjugated structures) and surface traps [6,43]. Using
τ
a v e r a g e
=
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 2
τ 2 2
/
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
, the average fluorescence lifetime (τ average) of N-CQDs was calculated as 4.43ns (χ 2 <1.017), which is shorter than other reported values [43]. The short fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs indicates the radiative recombination of the excitons causing the fluorescence [23,42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity by introducing N-CQDs in cells
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the cytotoxicity of as-prepared materials, MTT assays were carried out [11–15,35]. Therefore, A549 and MCF-7 cells were selected as representative cell lines to incubate with N-CQDs for 24h. As shown in Fig. 5 , the cell viability decreased by 13% and 17% respectively when the concentrations of N-CQDs reached up to 400μg/mL. These observations clearly indicated that the as-synthesized N-CQDs had exerted lesser cytotoxic effects on the cells. Furthermore, as the literature [11,35] examined, it is definite that the dose of N-CQDs have low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility for cell labeling and imaging after incubating 24h. Importantly, at the concentrations typically used in bioimaging applications (<200μg/mL), the cytotoxicity of the N-CQDs was found to be marginal.

As noted above, the N-CQDs as-synthesized in our method had exert any lesser cytotoxic effects on the cells. Simultaneously, with excellent water solubility, excitation-dependent emission properties and high fluorescence quantum yield, the N-CQDs may be an attractive alternative for living cells imaging. Fig. 6 shows the fluorescence microscopy images of MCF-7 cells after treating with 200μg/mL N-CQDs for 24h. Apparently, N-CQDs entered into the MCF-7 cells and displayed bright and colorful photoluminescence, while the shape and viability of cells show no substantial change, indicating the N-CQDs can label the cells of MCF-7. This observation clearly indicated that the N-CQDs present their candidature as an effective bio-labeling agent for living cells.

NADH is an important coenzyme involved in metabolic processes, which stimulates the energy production in every cell of living organisms (brain, muscles, heart and so on). Specifically, it alternates between the reduced state NADH and the oxidized state NAD+, thereby serving as a carrier of hydrogen and electrons in cellular respiration or photosynthesis [44–46]. The fluctuating levels of NADH in the living cells normally suggest an imbalance of metabolism. Therefore, it is significant and essential to develop a new strategy for the determination of NADH. We hereby delightfully report that we have accidentally come across this interesting fact that the addition of NADH could effectively quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs as-synthesized in our system.

To study the quenching mechanism involved and to comprehend the selectivity of the fluorescent assay towards NADH, the fluorescence responses of the proposed N-CQDs to 1mM NADH were compared with 1mM of other coenzymes, namely, nicotinamide, d-biotin, ATP, glutathione, folic acid and vitamin B1/B2/B6. As shown in Fig. 7 , no apparent fluorescence quenching effects of the solutions are observed. The interference of ATP to NADH is negligible and we can avoid the interference through other auxiliary methods [45,46].

The reason that ATP has a little quenching in the fluorescence signal possible ascribes to their similar chemical composition and spatial structure. Specifically, both NADH and ATP have cis-diols group, which can selectively lead to the esterification reaction with the carboxyl group on the surface of N-CQDs. It may impede the radiative recombination of electrons and holes trapped on the CQDs surface, causing the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs (Fig. 8 ). However, unlike NADH, the other coenzymes have no analogous unit, consequently, they cannot lead to the corresponding fluorescent quenching. Therefore, the N-CQDs can well recognize NADH from other coenzymes.

The analytical performance of the proposed fluorescence sensor to NADH was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 9a, the PL intensity of N-CQDs at 390nm decreased gradually with increasing concentrations of NADH, indicating that the addition of NADH can effectively quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs. Fig. 9b presents the PL intensity versus the concentration of NADH. A linear calibration plot in the concentration range of 0–80μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.993 is displayed and the detection limit down to 25.1nM is obtained based on a 3δ/slope. To our knowledge, the linear range and the detection limit are 5–150 and 10–200 times lower than those recently reported by other methods of NADH detection, respectively [44–46]. Therefore, a simple, green, and high sensitivity and selectivity non-enzymatic sensor for NADH has been established.

To further investigate the potential practical applications of this method, the sensing of NADH in human serum was carried out. A 20μL aliquot of pretreated serum samples with different concentrations of NADH were spiked in the N-CQDs system and reacted for 1h, and then the fluorescence intensity was measured. As shown in Table 1 , NADH concentrations obtained by the proposed method are well in agreement with the values added before, indicating the practicability of the proposed sensing platform.

We have developed a facile one-step synthetic approach for the preparation of ethylenediamine-passivated N-CQDs via hydrothermal carbonization for the first time. A more plausible mechanism of the photoluminescence from N-CQDs may be attributed to the interplay between the holes trapped and the passivation of the CQDs. Due to low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, the N-CQDs could enter into the MCF-7 cells while the shape and viability of cells showing no substantial change. Besides, a simple, green, highly sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor for NADH was established due to the esterification reaction of cis-diols group in NADH and carboxyl group on the surface of N-CQDs. It impeded the radiative recombination of electrons and holes trapped on the CQDs surface, causing the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21175114, 21305067), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK2011441, 20130754), PhD Fund of MOE for Young Teachers (0133219120019), Qing Lan project of Jiangsu Province, NJUST Research Dounding (No.2012ZDJH009), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (30920130112012), and a project founded by the priority academic program development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.05.006.

Fabrication of 2D ordered mesoporous carbon nitride and its use as electrochemical sensing platform for H2O2, nitrobenzene, and NADH detection

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Wen-Jun Niu is a Ph.D. student in School of Environmental and Biological Engineering, Nanjing University of Science & Technology, PR China. He received his MS in physical chemistry from Yangzhou University, China in 2013.
Ye Li is currently studying for degree of MS in School of Environmental and Biological Engineering, Nanjing University of Science & Technology, PR China.
Rong-Hui Zhu is currently studying for degree of MS in School of Environmental and Biological Engineering, Nanjing University of Science & Technology, PR China.

Dan Shan is presently employed as a professor in School of Environmental and Biological Engineering, Nanjing University of Science & Technology, PR China. She received her MS in physical chemistry from Yangzhou University, China in 2001, and her Ph.D. in electrochemistry from Joseph Fourier University of Grenoble (France) in 2004. She worked at School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, PR China from 2004 to 2011. Her current fields of interest include biosensor, interfacial electrochemistry and electroconductive polymer.
Yan-Rong Fan is an associate professor in School of Environmental and Biological Engineering, Nanjing University of Science & Technology, PR China.

Xue-Ji Zhang is a professor and Dean in School of Environmental and Biological Engineering, Nanjing University, PR China. He received his Ph.D. in electrochemistry from Wu Han University, PR China (1989). His current fields of interest include bio-analysis, electrochemical sensors and biosensors, microelectrodes, ultramicroelectrodes and nanoelectrodes, nanosensors, free radical sensors.
